segments.text: Good morning, Deborah. Welcome to the Education and Workforce Select Committee
hearing for the Immigration
Bill. We've got five minutes with you this morning, that includes time for
questions, and we'll hand over to you now.
Yes, good morning, Chair. Thank you.
Nice to see you again and the committee again.
Well, I just thought that I would, as I did last week, just
leave it open for questions.
I think that's the best use of the few minutes that we have together.
Wonderful. Yes, it is, especially given that we've got only five minutes.
Do we have any questions from the committee?
I've got one to start. I just wondered, we have heard a lot
from
all of our submitters really, the things that they obviously
don't like about the bill or do like about the bill. What would you change?
We want to hear the constructive stuff.
What would you change immediately?
Yeah. The focus would be on removing the humanitarian appeals for visitor visa
holders.
I don't think there's a policy case for that.
It's only going to affect
just over 100 people a year,
when over 400,000 visas are issued visitor
visas. And, there's a high success rate for
exceptional circumstances of a humanitarian nature that really affect
families that are really doing it tough in New Zealand.
And it's just going to move that
heat and stress in the system into ministerial appeals, to electorate
offices, to the media. It's just going to create a lot of noise and
disruption in the system, and
my submission would say there's no justification for that.
And one small follow-up question. Do you see that number escalating?
Because you said, it's only 100 a year.
That's now. If it were to be left as is,
do you see that number escalating?
Those numbers have been pretty stable, and the reason there's been a small
increase of a couple of dozen a month is because of the
COVID lockdowns where people were here for years and had families and kids and that
kind of thing. There's actually been a stable number,
relatively, to the numbers. And when we're talking about this
is the place, the specialist tribunal, that looks at our obligations
concerning rights of the children in those international obligations.
Why would we move such complexity for
such vulnerable cases in the community that
create such disruption for the rest of the system?
Why would we take it away from that specialist tribunal?
Mm. Thank you.
When there's been no business case for it. Yeah.
Thank you. And a question from Phil Twy.
Yeah. Thanks, Chair.
So Deborah, a couple of things.
Drawing on your experience as a practitioner, could you talk about,
and your knowledge of the data in terms of
the appeals that go to the IPT for these
humanitarian appeals against deportation.
What are the kinds of cases? What are the kinds of people and the kind of
situations that are in those numbers?
Yeah, sure. So first of all, this is a tough tribunal.
And so there's about a 40% success rate, which
means that's very high. And you've got cases
like, and 40% of the cases are
the Pasifika community. And they're cases like a family might have a
child with autism or special needs or a disability, and they really need that
auntie to stay because they've bonded with the kid, because they've been looking
after the child.
Or the grandparents of there supporting that family with those children with those
special needs. That seems to be a bit of a theme that I'm seeing coming
through.
Families with disability needs, needing those other families to
support them. Yeah, that's quite common.
Mm.
And,
Deborah, for the benefit of people who haven't had direct
engagement with the tribunal, could you just
talk a bit about
how the tribunal works, the quality of its decisions,
how it functions?
Yeah. So these appeals we're talking about, they're on the papers.
So we're not talking about court hearings.
The appellants have to pay quite a hefty fee, it's over $1,000 now, and that
can always be increased if we wanted to manage costs.
Appellants have to appeal within 42 days.
It's quite a narrow window, which is probably why a lot of people don't appeal.
And then it's a specialist tribunal.
It's internationally renowned. It's thorough, and it
applies New Zealand's international obligations to these cases.
So it's a place in New Zealand where we can have confidence that New
Zealand is meeting our international obligations.
So when we go to Geneva to report to the Human Rights Committee
and so on, and the Committee for the Rights of Children and People with
Disabilities, we know that that is a place in New Zealand that is looking
after and guarding those obligations for us.
Mm. Could you,
Katie, is that okay if I ask one more question?
If it can be concise. I'm just trying to come off speech-
Sure
... phone to, yep. Thank you.
Yeah. In these kinds of cases, Deborah, what are
New Zealand's international obligations?
Yeah, so they commonly involve the rights of the child, people with
disabilities, discrimination against women,
our fundamental human rights obligations in terms of
even sometimes living conditions.
It's about exceptional circumstances of a humanitarian
nature. And
human beings can be complicated and messy, and this is a place where
the tribunal can pick through all of the issues and consider whether
or not there's an exceptional case on humanitarian grounds.
And as I say,
it takes heat out of the system, so we don't have front page media
articles, electorate officers, ministers,
having to grapple with these very tough cases.
Thank you so much, Deborah. Really appreciate your participation in this process
and being available for us this morning.
Thank you.
And we'll move on now to our next submitter, who is Snow Ryan.
Snow, hopefully we've got you online and ready.
There we go.
Welcome. We have five minutes with you this morning.
That includes time for questions.
Hopefully, we can see you. We can't hear you yet.
We definitely see you there. You just need to turn your camera on and
unmute yourself.
There we go.
Good morning.
Ah, good morning, Snow. We have five minutes with you this morning, and that
includes time for questions. So I'll hand it over to you now.
We can't see you yet, but if you can turn your camera on, but we can definitely
hear you.
Just one moment. I'll try with the camera.
I'm having problems with the Zoom meeting this morning, so.
Ah, that's okay. The point is that we can hear you, so it's not the end of the
world if you wanted to carry on.
Wonderful. I'll carry on so I don't waste any more of your time.
Good morning, and thank you for the opportunity to speak this morning.
My name is Snow Ryan. I'm an everyday Kiwi.
I have submitted in writing on the specific provisions of this bill.
Today I want to use my five minutes differently, not to restate what
is in my written submission, but to place a concern before the
committee that I believe goes beyond the individual clauses.
My written submission raises proportionality, oversight, and
transparency. I stand by all of it.
But the deeper concern is this. The combined effect of these provisions
creates a structural architecture that comparable
democracies have demonstrated leads to serious harm.
And through intent-- No, sorry, not through intent, but through the
predictable logic of accumulated power without adequate
constraint.
The United Kingdom did not set out to wrongly detain and
deport British citizens. The Windrush scandal happened
because of an accumulation of individually defensible choices.
Extended liability periods, erosion of appeal rights,
data sharing without purpose limits, administrative detention
without judicial oversight.
The Windrush Independent Lessons Learned Review found that structural
features, not individual bad actors, were the primary drivers
of harm.
Every one of those structural features is present in this bill.
I have tabled a written addendum that sets out the case in a detail, which I
have not yet forwarded to the committee, and I would do so under the instruction of
the secretary.
But I want to name the most serious gap
directly. This bill does not establish statutory
flaws for immigration detention.
No maximum period before judicial review, no
prohibition on using prison facilities for
people who have committed no crime.
No duty to consider alternatives before detaining someone.
These are not technical omissions.
They are the conditions that enabled enforcement practices in the
United States and the United Kingdom that proved
extraordinarily difficult to reverse once normalized.
New Zealand has a narrow window to build different foundations.
That window is this committee and this bill.
I'm asking for three specific amendments.
Mandatory judicial review of detention within 72 hours,
explicit purpose limitation preventing enforcement agencies
from accessing data held by schools, health providers, and community
organizations, and a statutory review period of no
more than three years.
But I want to close with something larger.
This bill asks Parliament to make consequential decisions about the kind of
enforcement state New Zealand will become.
Decisions that will shape experience of Pasifika communities,
migrant workers, international students, and long-term residents for a
generation.
Those decisions deserve more than a select committee process and a
government majority.
I'm an advocate for citizens assemblies.
I believe this bill is precisely the kind of question a citizens
assembly exists to answer. Not because this committee cannot do its
job, but because the trade-offs between security, liberty, and the
kind of country we want to be are not technical questions.
They are democratic ones.
Ordinarily, New Zealanders, given time and evidence, are capable of
navigating that complexity. They have not been asked.
I urge the committee to consider recommending that the government convene a
citizens assembly on immigration enforcement powers before the
substantive provisions of this bill take effect.
That would not be a delay. It would be an act of democratic
confidence. New Zealand has historically done this well.
We can do it again.
Thank you, and I open for questions.
Thank you, Snow.
It doesn't appear that we have any questions for you, but it does not mean there's
a lack of interest at all. It means you've given us a really constructive and
helpful submission. So we thank you for that.
Wonderful. Thank you.
Thank you. We'll move on now to our next submitter, who we have New Zealand
Council for Civil Liberties.
For that, we have Stephen Enslin, and
hopefully we see you online. Yes, there we are. All right.
We've got 10 minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions.
We'll hand to you now.
Okay. Thank you very much. Good morning, everyone.
The New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties strongly objects to this
billMinister Stanford is quoted in Radio New Zealand article about
the council submission saying that the ministry did not intend to
limit people's freedom with this bill.
"Did not intend" is about the best thing that the minister can say from our
perspective.
First, it is admission that the bill does limit everyone's freedom,
and second, it is an admission of error.
The minister is telling this committee that the bill is broken and needs to be
fixed. As we noted in our written submission, the
Attorney General's Bill of Rights compliance report implies that the core
functions of immigration compliance are so important
that any limitation of our rights can be justified.
Mostly that's just another lazy report from the Attorney General, but it
does accidentally introduce the topics which are at the core of this debate,
which are:
how important are the core functions of immigration compliance?
And
what problem is this bill addressing?
The council believes that immigration compliance already have too many
powers, not that they have too few.
If we cut immigration compliance inside our borders entirely,
we would not merely be more free, we would be happier and more productive.
Our immigration officers are already going around and harassing people,
and more importantly from the council's perspective, interfering with our
hard-fought liberties.
The council's main point is that our civil liberties are vastly more important
than the core functions of immigration compliance.
The second issue is why does this bill exist?
The bill doesn't answer that question.
It says things like improving operations or increasing
effectiveness without the slightest awareness that those are not answers to the
question. Why do operations need improving?
And more to the point, where is the evidence that the principal act is
ineffective?
The regulatory impact statement does not answer the question either.
It merely says there is an opportunity to improve the
effectiveness of the compliance powers.
In short, the council does not believe that there is an effectiveness problem which
needs addressing, and we think that the committee should agree with us that the
minister has failed to present a compelling reason for change.
The council will not support any restrictive restrictions on the public's
freedoms for the purpose of immigration compliance, except at the border.
And the council remains steadfast in our belief that the existing power
imbalance at the border, where people effectively lose almost all of our
rights, is not sufficiently justified.
Civil liberties are under threat around the world.
Immigration enforcement is being used overseas as a
pretext for incarcerating people who have committed no offenses.
Given this context, we should be amending the Immigration Act to prevent
similar overreach from occurring here.
This bill does the opposite.
We ask the committee to recommend that this bill does not proceed.
I'll now open for questions.
Thank you. We have a question from Grant McCallum.
Yeah, got a couple here just quickly, Madam Chair.
First of all, thank you for your presentation.
I just want to establish first, do you think every country

Who could immigrate to it? Yes.
You do?
Yes.
Okay. And if people come here on a visa and
their visa runs out,
those people then should they leave? Or what should happen to them?
Yes, those people should leave indeed.
So-

We don't need a specialty police force running around the country.
Hold on. You think the police should go around and do this job?
Not immigration officers who are professionally trained in this space and

I think that police are also trained and understand the law.
Yeah, but immigration is quite a specific-
The council believes. Yeah.
All right. Thank you.
Okay. Thank you. And a question from Phil Twyford.

Can you talk a bit more about why the
evolution of immigration staff
into being given increasing numbers of

out
going to places of business, stopping everyone there who is a person of

Okay. Thank you.
Thank you very much.

We'll move on-
